qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: GraphQL asset-by-URL mutation allows SSRF to loopback and private hosts
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The advisory targets the GraphQL `@parseRefs` directive's missing authorization check in `craft\services\Elements::parseRefs` / `_getRefTokenReplacement`, while this finding targets the asset-mutation `_file.url` fetch path in `craft\gql\resolvers\mutations\Asset::handleUpload()` and its hostname validation. The shared GraphQL surface is only contextual; the vulnerable code, sink, and root cause differ.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described `@parseRefs` information-disclosure root cause. The only real final finding in this run is an SSRF in GraphQL asset-save mutations, so the judge must leave the advisory unmatched and skip PoC execution.
evidence: The advisory describes IDOR/information disclosure through the GraphQL `@parseRefs` directive because `craft\services\Elements::parseRefs` resolves element references and attributes without the expected authorization check.

The upstream fix commit `4d98a07e47580f1712095825d3e3c4d67bc9f8b9` makes `@parseRefs` optional per GraphQL schema, reinforcing that the affected component is directive handling rather than asset upload or remote fetch logic.
The session input manifest lists only one final qualifying finding: `/Users/lumi/ziyue/EvoHunt-Run/advisory_d3f3d032/graphql_asset_url_ssrf`.

That finding's report and finalized checklist place the issue in `GraphqlController` plus `craft\gql\resolvers\mutations\Asset::handleUpload()` for attacker-controlled `_file.url` SSRF, not in `Elements::parseRefs`, `_getRefTokenReplacement`, or any authorization bypass on element reference parsing.
